name: Sweden
summary: Sweden is the big, blue-and-yellow heart of Scandinavia — a long, skinny country that stretches roughly 1,000 miles from snowy Arctic reindeer country in the north down to gentle farm fields in the south.
content:
Sweden is the big, blue-and-yellow heart of Scandinavia — a long, skinny country that stretches roughly 1,000 miles from snowy Arctic reindeer country in the north down to gentle farm fields in the south. It's the fifth-largest country in Europe (about 174,000 square miles) but home to only around 10.6 million people, which means there's an enormous amount of forest, lake, and empty space per person. In fact, Sweden has over 100,000 lakes and, astonishingly, holds the world record for islands — well over 260,000 of them, though fewer than 1,000 are inhabited.

Sweden is a constitutional monarchy, which is a fancy way of saying it has a king who wears the crown but doesn't run the country. King Carl XVI Gustaf has been on the throne since 1973, but his job is basically ceremonial — cutting ribbons, hosting state dinners, and handing out the Nobel Prizes. The real power sits with the Riksdag, a 349-member parliament elected by the people, and the prime minister who leads the government. This system has made Sweden one of the most stable, least corrupt, and most equal societies on earth, with famously generous features like tax-funded schools and universal healthcare.

For a country its size, Sweden punches way above its weight in exports the whole world recognizes. There's IKEA, the flat-pack furniture giant founded by 17-year-old Ingvar Kamprad in 1943. There's Volvo, the Gothenburg carmaker that invented the modern three-point seatbelt in 1959 — and then gave the patent away for free so other companies could save lives too. There's the clothing brand H&M, the music streaming service Spotify (born in Stockholm in 2006), and of course ABBA, the four-person pop supergroup formed in 1972 that became one of the best-selling music acts in history. Sweden is actually one of the biggest music exporters on the planet per person — a lot of your favorite pop hits were secretly written by Swedish songwriters.

The language is Swedish, a musical, sing-songy cousin of Norwegian and Danish — the three are close enough that Scandinavians can often understand each other, a bit like how Spanish and Italian speakers can sometimes muddle through. Nearly everyone in Sweden also speaks excellent English, so the family won't have trouble getting around. A few words worth knowing: *tack* (thanks), *hej* (hello, said twice as *hej hej* for extra friendliness), and the all-important *fika* — the sacred Swedish coffee-and-pastry break that's less about caffeine and more about slowing down with friends.

A quick note on the Norway comparison, since the family is doing both: Sweden and Norway are neighbors and old rivals who were actually joined in a union under the same king from 1814 until 1905, when Norway peacefully went its own way (negotiations for that split happened in Karlstad, one of your stops). Geographically, Sweden is the flatter, foresty, lake-dotted one with the big Baltic archipelagos; Norway is the dramatic one with the deep fjords and steep mountains. Culturally they're like siblings — similar languages, similar love of nature and cozy comfort, and a friendly habit of teasing each other endlessly. Swedes are also traditionally Lutheran Christian, and while modern Sweden is quite secular, its calendar, its churches, and its holidays are still deeply shaped by that heritage.

**Fun fact for the kids:** Sweden takes the "right to roam" (called *allemansrätten*) so seriously that you're legally allowed to walk, camp, pick berries, and swim almost anywhere in nature — even on land someone else owns — as long as you're respectful and don't disturb people or their homes. Imagine your whole country being one giant, shared backyard.

name: Stockholm
summary: Stockholm is Sweden's capital and largest city, and it sits in one of the most beautiful settings of any capital in the world — spread across 14 islands right where the freshwater of Lake Mälaren spills into the salty Baltic Sea.
content:
Stockholm is Sweden's capital and largest city, and it sits in one of the most beautiful settings of any capital in the world — spread across 14 islands right where the freshwater of Lake Mälaren spills into the salty Baltic Sea. Those islands are stitched together by 57 bridges, and water is absolutely everywhere, which is why Stockholm is often nicknamed the "Venice of the North." You can actually travel between many of the islands by public ferry using the same ticket you'd use for the metro.

The city's official birthday is 1252, when a powerful statesman named Birger Jarl wrote two letters that are the first known mention of "Stockholm." Birger Jarl is traditionally credited as the founder, and he chose this spot for a very clever reason: whoever controlled this narrow watery gateway controlled all the trade flowing between the rich inland region and the Baltic. He built a fortress here to act as a "lock" on Lake Mälaren, protecting the interior from seaborne raiders. The name itself is a bit of a puzzle — *stock* means "log" and *holm* means "islet," and one old legend claims the Vikings decided where to build by floating a log bound in gold to see where it washed ashore.

From that fortified beginning, Stockholm grew fast. By the end of the 1200s it was Sweden's biggest town, thriving on the iron and copper trade with the German-run Hanseatic League. It officially became the capital in the 1400s–1600s era and has been the political and royal heart of Sweden ever since. Today the city proper has roughly 1 million residents, with well over 2 million in the wider metropolitan area — making it the largest city in all of Scandinavia.

Modern Stockholm is a fascinating mash-up of old and new. On one island you have Gamla Stan, the impossibly picturesque medieval Old Town with cobblestones and a royal palace; a short walk away you have sleek Scandinavian design shops, a booming tech-startup scene (according to VC firm Atomico's analysis of global unicorn distribution, Stockholm is the second most prolific tech hub in the world on a per-capita basis, with about 6.3 billion-dollar companies per million people, just behind Silicon Valley's 6.9), and one of the world's most artistic subway systems. Since the 1950s, more than 90 of the 100 metro stations have been decorated by over 150 artists with mosaics, sculptures, and paintings across the 110-km network, earning it the title "the world's longest art gallery." Every December, the city hosts the glittering Nobel Prize ceremony, the tradition set up in the will of Swedish inventor Alfred Nobel, who was born here.

**Fun fact for the kids:** Because Stockholm sits so far north, summer days are wildly long — in June and July you can get around 18 hours of daylight, with the sky never getting fully dark. In deep winter it flips the other way, with as little as 6 hours of daylight. Visiting in late July, the family will get those magical, endless bright evenings perfect for late-night ice cream by the water.

name: Gamla Stan
summary: Gamla Stan — literally "the Old Town" — is where Stockholm was born back in 1252, and it's one of the largest and best-preserved medieval city centers in all of Europe.
content:
Gamla Stan — literally "the Old Town" — is where Stockholm was born back in 1252, and it's one of the largest and best-preserved medieval city centers in all of Europe. Sitting mostly on the little island of Stadsholmen, it's a car-light maze of narrow cobblestone lanes, ochre-and-rust-colored merchant houses, ancient churches, and hidden courtyards that have been in continuous daily use for more than 700 years. Unlike a lot of "old towns" that are really reconstructions, Gamla Stan is the real deal — a living neighborhood layered over genuine medieval bones. Its street plan is still the 13th-century one, and beneath many buildings you'll find original medieval cellar vaults.

The beating heart of the district is Stortorget, the oldest square in Stockholm, ringed by those famous tall, skinny, candy-colored houses you see on every postcard. But this cheerful square hides one of the darkest stories in Swedish history: the Stockholm Bloodbath of November 1520. Here's what happened. The Danish king Christian II had just been crowned king of Sweden (Sweden and Denmark were then joined in the uneasy Kalmar Union). After days of feasting, Christian suddenly turned on his Swedish guests, accusing around 80–90 nobles, bishops, and leading citizens of heresy — and over roughly three days had them beheaded right there in the square. It was meant to crush Swedish resistance forever. Instead, it backfired spectacularly: one of the victims was the father of a young nobleman named Gustav Vasa, who escaped, raised a rebellion, drove out the Danes, and became king of an independent Sweden in 1523. In a very real sense, the massacre in this pretty square accidentally gave birth to the Swedish nation.

Wandering the lanes, keep an eye out for Mårten Trotzigs Gränd, the narrowest street in all of Stockholm — just 90 centimeters (about 35 inches) wide at its skinniest point, a squeeze-through staircase alley named after a 16th-century German merchant who became one of the city's richest men (before being murdered, possibly robbed, on a trip to his mine). There's also a legend that a white stone set into one building on Stortorget marks a cannonball fired during those turbulent times.

The architecture here is distinctly North German brick-Gothic, a legacy of the Hanseatic League merchants who shaped early Stockholm. Ironically, this gorgeous district was considered a run-down slum from the mid-1800s to mid-1900s; serious preservation only began in earnest in the 20th century, and now it's one of the most desirable — and most visited — places in Scandinavia.

**Fun fact for the kids:** The whole Old Town is small enough (about 800 by 400 meters) that you can walk from any point to any other in about 10 minutes — but it's so twisty that even guidebook-toting tourists happily get lost. Getting lost here is half the fun.

name: Vasa Museum / the Vasa Warship
summary: The Vasa Museum on the island of Djurgården houses one of the most jaw-dropping objects you will ever see: an almost completely intact wooden warship that sank in 1628 and spent 333 years at the bottom of Stockholm's harbor.
content:
The Vasa Museum on the island of Djurgården houses one of the most jaw-dropping objects you will ever see: an almost completely intact wooden warship that sank in 1628 and spent 333 years at the bottom of Stockholm's harbor. It is the only preserved 17th-century ship in the world, and standing beneath its towering, carved hull in the museum's dim light is genuinely awe-inspiring — like meeting a ghost from four centuries ago.

Here's the story. In the 1620s, Sweden's warrior-king Gustavus Adolphus was at war with Poland-Lithuania and wanted a magnificent flagship to show off Sweden's growing power. The result was *Vasa*, built between 1626 and 1628, bristling with 64 bronze cannons on two gun decks and dripping with hundreds of elaborate painted and gilded sculptures meant to strike fear (and envy) into everyone who saw it. There was just one problem: it was dangerously top-heavy. There wasn't enough ballast down low to balance all those heavy guns and carvings up high. Alarmingly, a stability test — where 30 sailors ran back and forth across the deck — had to be stopped early because the ship rocked so violently it looked ready to capsize. But the king was impatient, and nobody dared tell him his dream ship was a floating disaster.

On August 10, 1628, *Vasa* set sail on its maiden voyage with crowds and foreign dignitaries watching from shore. It managed to travel barely 1,300 meters (about three-quarters of a mile) before a gust of wind tipped it over, water poured in through the open gun ports, and the mighty warship sank in front of the horrified public. Around 30 people drowned. A furious king demanded to know who was to blame; an inquest was held, but in the end no one was punished.

Then came the miracle of preservation. The Baltic Sea is cold and low in salt, which means it doesn't host the wood-munching shipworms that would have devoured the ship in warmer oceans. So *Vasa* just waited, buried in mud, remarkably intact. In the 1950s, an amateur marine archaeologist named Anders Franzén doggedly hunted for the wreck and found it in 1956. In a daring salvage operation, divers made over 1,300 dives to tunnel beneath the hull and thread steel cables under it, and on April 24, 1961, *Vasa* finally broke the surface after 333 years. Something like 95% of the ship was original. It was then sprayed for years with a waxy chemical (polyethylene glycol) to keep the waterlogged wood from cracking as it dried. Along with the ship, divers recovered tens of thousands of objects — clothing, tools, coins, even sailors' skeletons — a complete time-capsule snapshot of 17th-century life.

By the beginning of 2025, *Vasa* had been seen by over 45 million people across the museum's history, and it draws well over a million visitors a year (about four in five from abroad). Business schools even teach the "Vasa syndrome" as a classic case of what happens when leaders ignore expert warnings and rush a project to please the boss.

**Fun fact for the kids:** Among the wild ideas floated for raising the ship was filling the hull with ping-pong balls to make it bob to the surface. (The engineers went with cables and pontoons instead.)

name: Dalarna
summary: If you asked a Swede to point to the single region that feels most deeply, soulfully *Swedish*, most would point to Dalarna.
content:
If you asked a Swede to point to the single region that feels most deeply, soulfully *Swedish*, most would point to Dalarna. Often called "Sweden in miniature," this central region packs the whole country into one place — shimmering lakes, deep forests, rolling farmland, and mountains rising toward the Norwegian border. But Dalarna is far more than pretty scenery. It's the cultural and emotional heartland of the nation, the place where the red cottages, the folk music, the midsummer maypole dances, and the hand-carved wooden horses all feel most alive and authentic.

Dalarna's outsized place in the Swedish soul comes largely from one dramatic chapter of history. In the winter of 1520–21, right after the Stockholm Bloodbath (in which his own father was executed), the young nobleman Gustav Vasa fled here as a fugitive, desperately trying to rally the independent-minded miners and farmers of Dalarna to rebel against Danish rule. At first, the people of Mora turned him down, and a discouraged Gustav set off on skis toward Norway and safety. But then the locals changed their minds — word had spread that the Danish king planned to raise taxes — and they sent their two fastest skiers racing after him, catching him near Sälen and persuading him to come back and lead the revolt. He did, and in 1523 he became king of an independent Sweden. That legendary ski-chase is the origin of the **Vasaloppet**, the world's oldest and largest long-distance cross-country ski race, first run in 1922. Every year on the first Sunday in March, tens of thousands of skiers cover the 90-kilometer course from Sälen to Mora (the reverse of Gustav's escape route), passing under a finish-line arch inscribed with the motto "In our forefathers' tracks for future victories." On September 2, 2025, the Swedish government's official cultural-canon report ("En kulturkanon för Sverige," SOU 2025:92) named the Vasaloppet the only sporting event among its 100 works, describing it as "a ski race that uniquely combines history, sports, outdoor life, and culture."

The region is also the birthplace of the icons foreigners most associate with Sweden. The bright red paint on those classic wooden cottages — *Falu rödfärg* — comes from the pigment of Dalarna's famous Falun copper mine. The carved and painted Dala horse (*dalahäst*) originated in the villages around Mora. The swirling floral folk-painting style called *kurbits* decorates everything from furniture to phone booths here. And Dalarna guards Sweden's oldest midsummer traditions more fiercely than anywhere else, with villagers dressing in traditional folk costume to raise flower-covered maypoles and dance late into the bright June night.

Dalarna has a long history of fierce independence — the same miners and farmers who backed Gustav Vasa also rose up against overlords repeatedly across the centuries, from Engelbrekt's rebellion in 1434 onward. The region even preserved its own runic alphabet (the "Dalecarlian runes") into the 1800s, long after the rest of Europe had forgotten such writing.

**Fun fact for the kids:** Dalarna is dotted with giant Dala horses, and the world's largest one stands about 13 meters (43 feet) tall in the town of Avesta — a horse bigger than a house.

name: Stockholm Archipelago
summary: Just beyond Stockholm's eastern edge, the solid city dissolves into water and rock, and one of the most extraordinary seascapes in Europe begins.
content:
Just beyond Stockholm's eastern edge, the solid city dissolves into water and rock, and one of the most extraordinary seascapes in Europe begins. The Stockholm Archipelago is a vast scattering of islands — commonly said to be around 30,000 islands, islets, and skerries (though careful counts put it at slightly over 24,000) — sprinkled across roughly 1,700 square kilometers of the Baltic Sea, stretching about 80 kilometers out toward Finland. National Geographic named it to its "Best of the World 2025" list (announced October 22, 2024), highlighting the new 168-mile Stockholm Archipelago Trail that links about 20 islands.

The whole landscape is a product of the last Ice Age. Glaciers scoured and pressed down the land, and when the ice melted, the crust slowly began to rebound — a process still happening today, with the islands rising by roughly a few millimeters every year. That's why the inner islands are green and forested with pine, birch, and alder, while the outermost ones are bare, wind-and-wave-polished lumps of granite. Locals call the whole thing the *skärgården* — literally the "skerry garden" — a name that hints at how tamed and beloved this wild place feels to Swedes.

For Stockholmers, the archipelago is practically a way of life. Once home to hardscrabble farmers and fishermen, it's now dotted with some 50,000 summer cottages, ranging from tiny red-painted one-room cabins to grand villas. In summer, the whole city seems to decamp to the islands to swim off the rocks, sail, pick wild berries, and eat fresh fish in the long golden evenings. Thanks to the "right to roam" law, anyone can go ashore and explore most uninhabited islands freely. The best part for visitors is how easy it is: regular public ferries (Waxholmsbolaget and the Cinderella boats) run from central Stockholm out to the islands for about the price of a casual lunch. Even travel guru Rick Steves calls it the single best day trip you can take from a European capital, recommending you simply stretch out on the ferry's top deck and soak it in.

name: Sandhamn
summary: Out on the far fringe of the Stockholm Archipelago — the last real stop before the open Baltic — sits the charming village of Sandhamn, on the island of Sandön ("Sand Island").
content:
Out on the far fringe of the Stockholm Archipelago — the last real stop before the open Baltic — sits the charming village of Sandhamn, on the island of Sandön ("Sand Island"). It's one of the archipelago's best natural harbors, and for centuries it has had two very different jobs: hardworking maritime outpost and glamorous sailing playground.

Sandhamn's working history goes back a long way. During the reign of King Charles XI in the late 1600s, the shipping passage here was opened to commercial traffic, and the island became an important customs and sea-pilot station — the skilled local pilots who guided big ships safely through the tricky archipelago waters. By the mid-1800s, piloting had become the main livelihood, and the customs house built in 1752 still stands as one of the island's landmark buildings. (Rough times came too: the entire island was burned down during a Russian raid in 1719, then rebuilt.)

Then, in the late 1800s, Sandhamn transformed into a fashionable summer resort. A regular steamship link to Stockholm arrived in 1865, and wealthy city-dwellers began building summerhouses. The turning point for sailing came in 1897, when the Royal Swedish Yacht Club (KSSS) — founded in 1830 and the oldest yacht club in continental Europe — inaugurated its Sandhamn clubhouse, a ceremony attended by King Oscar II himself. Ever since, Sandhamn has been the epicenter of Swedish yachting. Today it's the finish (and traditionally the start) of the **Round Gotland Race** (*Gotland Runt*), first run in 1937 and described by the KSSS as the world's largest annual offshore race — a two-day regatta that averages around 300 boats sailing roughly 350 nautical miles around the island of Gotland. Rick Steves compares Sandhamn to Nantucket — half ritzy nautical party scene near the yacht club, half a quieter, storybook old town of colorful wooden cottages tucked among the pines.

**Fun fact for the kids:** Only about a hundred people live on Sandhamn year-round, but in summer that swells to 2,000–3,000 residents plus around 100,000 visitors. The island is also the setting for a popular Swedish crime-novel series and TV show, "The Sandhamn Murders."

name: Storkyrkan / Stockholm Cathedral
summary: Standing right next to the Royal Palace in the heart of Gamla Stan, Storkyrkan (literally "the Great Church") is the oldest church in Stockholm, with origins in the 13th century — tradition credits its founding to Birger Jarl, and the oldest written mention dates to 1279.
content:
Standing right next to the Royal Palace in the heart of Gamla Stan, Storkyrkan (literally "the Great Church") is the oldest church in Stockholm, with origins in the 13th century — tradition credits its founding to Birger Jarl, and the oldest written mention dates to 1279. It's Sweden's royal cathedral, the setting for centuries of coronations, royal weddings, and state ceremonies, right up to the present day. Its towering clock tower, added in the 1740s and rising about 66 meters (216 feet), is a handy landmark for finding your way around the Old Town — just look up. Though the church has medieval Gothic bones, its exterior was given a Baroque makeover in the 1600s to match the neighboring palace.

The cathedral's greatest treasure is one of the most spectacular pieces of medieval art in Northern Europe: the wooden sculpture group of **Saint George and the Dragon**, inaugurated in 1489 and attributed to the master sculptor Bernt Notke of Lübeck. This isn't a small statue — it's a dramatic, larger-than-life scene of a golden-armored knight on horseback plunging his weapon into a writhing dragon, with a rescued princess nearby. It was commissioned by the Swedish regent Sten Sture the Elder to celebrate his victory over the Danes at the Battle of Brunkeberg in 1471, and it works on two levels: it's a religious image of good defeating evil, but it's also political propaganda — George stands for Sten Sture and Sweden, the dragon for the defeated Danish king, and the princess for the saved city of Stockholm.

Notke pulled out all the stops, carving the piece mostly from oak but adding real materials for extra realism and shock value: metal, leather, actual human hair, and — for the dragon's spiky armor — genuine elk antlers. The knight even wears a box of relics said to contain real bones of Saint George. It's been called the artistic high point of Notke's whole career. During World War II, the Swedes were so worried about losing this masterpiece that they secretly disassembled it in 1940 and hid it in an underground subway tunnel for safekeeping.

**Fun fact for the kids:** Storkyrkan is also home to the *Vädersolstavlan* ("Sun Dog Painting"), which shows a strange halo phenomenon of glowing rings and multiple "suns" spotted in the sky over Stockholm in 1535. The version hanging there is a 1636 copy, and it contains the oldest known picture of the city of Stockholm.

The version hanging there is a 1636 copy, and it contains the oldest known picture of the city of Stockholm.

name: The Royal Palace of Stockholm & Changing of the Guard
summary: The Royal Palace (Kungliga Slottet) in Gamla Stan is one of the largest palaces in Europe still in use by a head of state — a colossal Baroque building said to contain over 600 rooms (some sources say 605, which fans love to point out is one more than Buckingham Palace) spread across seven floors.
content:
The Royal Palace (Kungliga Slottet) in Gamla Stan is one of the largest palaces in Europe still in use by a head of state — a colossal Baroque building said to contain over 600 rooms (some sources say 605, which fans love to point out is one more than Buckingham Palace) spread across seven floors. It's the official residence of the King of Sweden and the working headquarters of the monarchy, where the royal court's roughly 200 staff handle state visits and ceremonies.

The palace has a fiery backstory. For centuries a medieval fortress called Tre Kronor ("Three Crowns") stood on this spot, but in 1697 it burned to the ground. During the chaos, people frantically threw valuables out the windows to save them — and tragically, one person was killed by falling objects, though no one died in the fire itself. King Charles XI's architect Nicodemus Tessin the Younger designed a grand new Italian-Baroque palace to replace it, and construction stretched across much of the 1700s.

Here's a fact that surprises many visitors: the royal family doesn't actually *live* here. Since 1981, King Carl XVI Gustaf and his family have made their home at Drottningholm Palace, a fairy-tale estate on an island in Lake Mälaren about 10 kilometers west of the city (and itself a UNESCO World Heritage Site). The Stockholm palace is more like the monarchy's grand downtown office and showpiece. Visitors can tour the opulent state apartments and several museums under one roof, including the Treasury (with the Swedish crown jewels), the Tre Kronor Museum (telling the story of the lost medieval castle), Gustav III's Museum of Antiquities, and the Royal Armoury with its coronation carriages, armor, and costumes.

The big daily crowd-pleaser is the **Changing of the Guard** ceremony in the outer courtyard — free to watch, full of marching soldiers, and on many summer days accompanied by a military band on horseback that has been known to play everything from traditional marches to ABBA and Queen. It typically starts around 12:15 on weekdays (13:15 on Sundays and holidays) and lasts about 40 minutes.

**Fun fact for the kids:** At the Royal Armoury, kids can try on knights' helmets and dress-up costumes, and peek at real royal carriages — one of the more hands-on stops in the palace for younger visitors.
fun_fact: At the Royal Armoury, kids can try on knights' helmets and dress-up costumes, and peek at real royal carriages — one of the more hands-on stops in the palace for younger visitors.

name: Skansen
summary: Skansen, on the leafy island of Djurgården, is the world's very first open-air museum — and after more than 130 years it's still one of the best.
content:
Skansen, on the leafy island of Djurgården, is the world's very first open-air museum — and after more than 130 years it's still one of the best. It was founded in 1891 by Artur Hazelius, a teacher and folklore enthusiast who was alarmed that Sweden's traditional rural way of life was vanishing fast as the country industrialized. His radical idea was that you shouldn't just put old objects in glass cases; you should preserve the whole living world they came from. So he traveled the country buying up around 150 historic buildings — farmhouses, workshops, a church, even a manor house — and had them dismantled, shipped piece by piece to Djurgården, and rebuilt. The oldest building on site dates all the way back to the 14th century.

The result is like stepping into a time machine that lets you walk through 500 years of Swedish life. Costumed interpreters play the roles of blacksmiths, bakers, glassblowers, and farmwives, doing real crafts and chores exactly as people did centuries ago — so you can smell the woodsmoke, hear the blacksmith's hammer, and taste bread baked in a period oven. Hazelius's invention was so influential that the word "skansen" became a generic term for open-air museums across Europe and beyond, and the model inspired similar living-history museums all over the world.

Skansen is also home to a wonderful zoo — the only zoo in central Stockholm — focused on Nordic animals like brown bears, moose, wolves, lynx, wolverines, reindeer, seals, and majestic owls. There's a petting-zoo area ("Lill-Skansen") where younger kids can meet friendly farm animals like goats, pigs, and rabbits. Throughout the year the museum hosts Sweden's beloved seasonal celebrations — Midsummer dancing, Lucia processions, and a Christmas market that's been running since 1903.

**Fun fact for the kids:** When people gave Hazelius exotic, non-Swedish animals as gifts, they didn't really fit his "miniature Sweden" idea — but he kept them anyway because he knew visitors (especially children) would be thrilled to see them. Some things never change.

name: Mora
summary: Mora is a friendly town of roughly 13,000 people set on a narrow neck of land in the heart of Dalarna, wedged between the northwestern end of Lake Siljan and the Österdalälven river.
content:
Mora is a friendly town of roughly 13,000 people set on a narrow neck of land in the heart of Dalarna, wedged between the northwestern end of Lake Siljan and the Österdalälven river. It's the cultural anchor of the whole Siljan lake district, and it wears three big claims to fame with quiet pride.

First, it's the legendary heart of the Gustav Vasa story. This is the town where, in 1520–21, Gustav Vasa begged the locals to join his rebellion, was turned down, and then was chased down by skiers and brought back to lead the fight for Swedish independence. That legend lives on today as the **Vasaloppet**, the world's oldest and largest cross-country ski race — and Mora is where it all comes to a head, at the famous finish line under the "In our forefathers' tracks for future victories" arch. Beside the finish stands Anders Zorn's 1903 bronze statue of Gustav Vasa. During race week, an estimated 100,000 people flood into town.

Second, Mora is the birthplace and lifelong home of **Anders Zorn** (1860–1920), one of Sweden's greatest painters, whose house and museum are among the town's top attractions (see the Zorn Museum entry). Third, the villages just outside Mora — especially Nusnäs — are the traditional home of the hand-carved Dala horse, Sweden's national symbol.

Mora has a genuine, unpretentious small-town character: red wooden houses, lakeside beaches for summer swimming, a giant Dala horse near the town center that stands about 2.4 meters (8 feet) tall, and a strong tradition of folk music, midsummer dancing, and craftsmanship. It makes a perfect, authentic base for the family to experience the real Dalarna.

**Fun fact for the kids:** Mora even has "Santa World" (Tomteland), a fairy-tale-themed park nearby where Christmas lives all year round.
fun_fact: Mora even has "Santa World" (Tomteland), a fairy-tale-themed park nearby where Christmas lives all year round.

name: Nusnäs & the Dala Horse Tradition
summary: The tiny lakeside village of Nusnäs, about 7 kilometers southeast of Mora on the shore of Lake Siljan, is the birthplace of Sweden's most beloved souvenir and national symbol: the *dalahäst*, or Dala horse.
content:
The tiny lakeside village of Nusnäs, about 7 kilometers southeast of Mora on the shore of Lake Siljan, is the birthplace of Sweden's most beloved souvenir and national symbol: the *dalahäst*, or Dala horse. This is where those cheerful, chunky, hand-painted red wooden horses actually come from — and you can still watch them being made by hand in the very same village where the tradition took off.

The carving of wooden horses in Dalarna goes back centuries. As early as the 1700s, lumberjacks and forest workers would sit by the fire on long, dark winter nights whittling toys for their children out of leftover scraps of wood. A horse was the natural choice — horses were essential, valuable animals, symbols of strength and faithfulness. Over time the horses grew more decorative, painted first in solid colors and then adorned with the swirling floral *kurbits* folk-painting style borrowed from local furniture decoration.

Nusnäs became the world capital of the craft in the 1920s thanks to two family workshops that still stand practically side by side today. **Grannas A. Olssons Hemslöjd**, founded in 1922 by Anders Olsson, is the oldest surviving Dala horse company in the world. **Nils Olssons Hemslöjd** was founded in 1928 by teenage brothers Nils and Jannes Olsson (just 15 and 13 years old), who borrowed a small sum to start carving horses after school to help their poor family. Both workshops still make every horse by hand from slow-growing local pine, and the family can watch the whole process: a horse is stamped and cut on a band saw, hand-carved with knives (so no two are ever exactly alike), dipped in primer to reveal flaws, sanded smooth, dipped in its base color, and finally decorated freehand by skilled "ripple" painters before a coat of varnish. Each horse passes through as many as nine pairs of hands.

The classic deep-red color comes from *Falu rödfärg* — the very same iron-rich red pigment that comes from the Falun copper mine and paints cottages all across Sweden, tying the little horse directly to the region's mining heritage. The Dala horse went global in stages: it was shown as Swedish folk art at the 1937 Paris Expo, and then a giant Dala horse at the **1939 New York World's Fair** captured international imaginations and cemented the little horse as *the* symbol of Sweden.

**Fun fact for the kids:** At the workshops you can often buy a pre-cut wooden horse blank to carve and paint your own — the perfect hands-on souvenir. And the artisans have a playful streak: keep an eye out for their "Dala pigs," "Dala elephants," and other cheeky twists on the classic.
